| | Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw
---
Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18]

| Friends--
Join us this weekend as we call on Congress to stop the healthcare heist! Due to weather, this event has been moved to December 6th.
December 6th: Health Care Heist: Taking it to the People
Stop the Healthcare Heist! Join us at this busy intersection for Part 2 of our action, where we're fighting to extend critical ACA credits and make healthcare truly affordable. Your voice is the missing piece--come stand with us!

name: Vikram Mansharamani
subject: 🤔 Mansharamani Musings: Vikram's November Thoughts 💭
body:
Read my current insights and analyses

| | | | | | | [View this email in your browser](https://mailchi.mp/mansharamani/vikram-update-november?e=93b2c2b824)
---
[|
---](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=f392f38c51&e=93b2c2b824)
| | | | | |
# Mansharamani Musings
---
| | November included time spent meeting with business executives, government leaders, academics, investment professionals, entrepreneurs, and journalists around North America. And while I did that, US-China relations continued their rollercoaster, art markets set new records, and the cost of Thanksgiving became the latest political debate. Links to my November posts below!
Happy December!
[_B_](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=0ff8ae8735&e=93b2c2b824) _est,**
Vikram**_

---
[|
---](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=65cabae863&e=93b2c2b824)
| | NOVEMBER 2, 2025

I have been traveling quite a bit lately. The chaos of airports and dependably undependable airlines seem to be the only certainty in an increasingly uncertain world. In fact, I now plan on at least a delay if not outright cancellation and arrive at the airport with backup plans for each trip, and I regularly travel in sneakers just so I’m prepared to sprint to a connecting flight. There was once a time that traveling by plane was glamorous, when travelers dressed for the occasion, and passengers could look forward to a good meal while en route to their destination. Those days are long gone, but might they return?
The question hints at a topic that’s long been a fascination of mine – cycles. In this case, we’re not talking about credit cycles that result in boom-bust dynamics, but instead of social and political cycles that change our day-to-day existence and feelings.

Those who know me know that I love reading. I continue to either listen to or read at least three books a month and am often looking for connections across seemingly disparate domains. My interest in social cycles began...
---
| [CLICK HERE TO CONTINUE READING](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=0c5e8c1508&e=93b2c2b824)
---
|
---
[|
---](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=7d8907425c&e=93b2c2b824)
| | NOVEMBER 9, 2025

This week was another spent on the road, and another spent dealing with cancellations and delays. So, as I cooled my jets in airport lounges waiting for the airlines to get their act together, I got caught up on recent political headlines.
The off-year elections were held on Tuesday, and it surely seems like it was a very good day for the Democrats. While the media has been trumpeting this as a harbinger for the looming mid-terms, I don’t know how much we should really read into Democrat wins in..., well, Democrat areas. New York City? New Jersey? Hardly Red bastions. But the margins of victory are still noteworthy. The Democrat gubernatorial candidate received nearly 60 percent of the vote in [New Jersey](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=3be4f2733a&e=93b2c2b824) as a super-charged Democrat base came out to vote in droves.
Looking forward, as a general rule, the party that holds the White House generally does poorly in the mid-terms. This was true in Virginia...
---
| [CLICK HERE TO CONTINUE READING](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=c9dfa673f6&e=93b2c2b824)
---
|
---
[|
---](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=9ff6157bdb&e=93b2c2b824)
| | NOVEMBER 16, 2025

Energy, which has usually meant oil, has been a driver of geopolitics for the past 100 years or so. As the internal combustion engine touched every aspect of life, fuel was needed for cars and trucks, industrial machines, and farm equipment. The world’s militaries transitioned away from coal-fired power and grew dependent upon gasoline to power tanks, planes, and ships. As eloquently described in Daniel Yergin’s [_**The Prize: The Epic Quest for Oil, Money & Power**_](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=1d41036d05&e=93b2c2b824) _,_ the pursuit of oil impacted virtually every one of a country’s actions in both times of war and times of peace.
A similar dynamic is playing out with rare earths and critical minerals. It’s becoming increasingly clear that these commodities are...
---
| [CLICK HERE TO CONTINUE READING](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=9b16d0e9db&e=93b2c2b824)
---
|
---
[|
---](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=4e6ad44c88&e=93b2c2b824)
| | NOVEMBER 23, 2025

Gustav Klimt’s “[Portrait of Elisabeth Lederer](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=71b1541b63&e=93b2c2b824)” [sold](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=6967fed430&e=93b2c2b824) this past week for a record $236.4 million at a Sotheby’s auction in New York. Like many, the transaction price caught my attention. But it also sent my “bubble-spotting” seismograph gyrating, elevating the probability of a possibly imminent asset earthquake.
As I’ve previously written, record-setting art prices have been a pretty reliable predictor of bubbles about to burst. Regular readers of my work know that I am a staunch advocate of multi-disciplinary thinking. My thinking is simple: if every lens is biased, limited, and incomplete, why not use multiple lenses? Such multi-lens thinking is the essence...
---
| [CLICK HERE TO CONTINUE READING](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=eb328f77e7&e=93b2c2b824)
---
|
---
[|
---](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=2f672db66c&e=93b2c2b824)
| | NOVEMBER 26, 2025

Thanksgiving is a time of traditions: driving or flying to visit family and friends, watching the Macy’s Thanksgiving Day Parade, strategizing for Black Friday sales, and of course, enjoying a turkey dinner. Another tradition, rumored to have begun with President Truman, is [the annual pardoning of turkeys by the US President](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=75653709f1&e=93b2c2b824). And this year was no different when President [Trump pardoned Gobble and Waddle](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=2297634a01&e=93b2c2b824).

More importantly, Trump made reference to the price of turkeys, noting that they had fallen dramatically. Inflation has been a major political and economic issue over the past few years, but it now seems like every single holiday is seized by political opponents to describe how bad inflation really is (or isn’t). What’s the cost of hot dogs...
---
| [CLICK HERE TO CONTINUE READING](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=a8f871792e&e=93b2c2b824)
---
|
---
|
---
[|
---](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=91c5a6191d&e=93b2c2b824)
| |
# NAVIGATING UNCERTAINTY
---
| | As many of you are aware, I’ve returned to writing my weekly newsletter and have been pleased by the rapidly growing interest in my thoughts. As I have in the past, I’m addressing a wide range of topics, ranging from geopolitics and economics to social trends to technology. The overall theme of my work is focused on “navigating uncertainty” and how a generalist approach can help us all make sense of what seems like chaos.
If you have not already done so, please consider subscribing!
---
| [Subscribe to “Navigating Uncertainty” by Vikram Mansharamani](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=27bb09e98c&e=93b2c2b824)
---
|
---
| |
# Want Vikram to speak at your event?
---
[|
---](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=8cf895be88&e=93b2c2b824)
| | Have an upcoming board meeting, corporate offsite, conference or other event in which you might want to have Vikram share his view about the global economy, geopolitical dynamics, technological developments, or social pressures? Click below to inquire about his availability!
---
| [BOOK VIKRAM](mailto:vikram@mansharamani.com?subject=SPEAKING%20INQUIRY&body=)
---
| |
# About Vikram
---
[|
---](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=7f675ef3dc&e=93b2c2b824)
| | **VIKRAM MANSHARAMANI** is an entrepreneur, consultant, scholar, neighbor, husband, father, volunteer, and professional generalist who thinks in multiple-dimensions and looks beyond the short-term. Self-taught to think around corners and connect original dots, he spends his time speaking with global leaders in business, government, academia, and journalism. **He’s currently the Chairman and CEO of Goodwell Foods, a manufacturer of private label frozen pizza**. _LinkedIn_ has twice listed him as its #1 Top Voice in Money & Finance, and _Worth_ profiled him as one of the 100 Most Powerful People in Global Finance. Vikram earned a PhD From MIT, has taught at Yale and Harvard, and is the author of three books, [ _**The Making of a Generalist: An Independent Thinker Finds Unconventional Success in an Uncertain World**_](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=9a77a059ab&e=93b2c2b824) , [ _**Think for Yourself: Restoring Common Sense in an Age of Experts and Artificial Intelligence**_](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=b5ef839479&e=93b2c2b824) and [ _**Boombustology: Spotting Financial Bubbles Before They Burst**_](https://mansharamani.us4.list-manage.com/track/click?u=caf139d4582deef486da11685&id=fd23b7baf0&e=93b2c2b824). Vikram lives in Lincoln, New Hampshire with his wife and two children, where they can usually be found hiking or skiing.
---

# [The Trump Accounts](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=287304&post_id=180574291&utm_source=post-email-title&utm_campaign=email-post-title&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwNTc0MjkxLCJpYXQiOjE3NjQ3NjcwMzMsImV4cCI6MTc2NzM1OTAzMywiaXNzIjoicHViLTI4NzMwNCIsInN1YiI6InBvc3QtcmVhY3Rpb24ifQ.Z_iUCAwbfydI6DSs81jy-kIFBWfOA7xAtL6NPokIVc4)

| Dec 3

| | | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=287304&post_id=180574291&utm_source=substack&isFreemail=true&submitLike=true&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwNTc0MjkxLCJyZWFjdGlvbiI6IuKdpCIsImlhdCI6MTc2NDc2NzAzMywiZXhwIjoxNzY3MzU5MDMzLCJpc3MiOiJwdWItMjg3MzA0Iiwic3ViIjoicmVhY3Rpb24ifQ.3Gg_IsQZkRlqh9xuS9IDy8svcpvYIhNOjQKJVE4MtMY&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-reaction&r=1hvr60)

| | [](https://substack.com/app-link/post?publication_id=287304&post_id=180574291&utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&action=share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=1hvr60&token=eyJ1c2VyX2lkIjo5MDUwMTE5MiwicG9zdF9pZCI6MTgwNTc0MjkxLCJpYXQiOjE3NjQ3NjcwMzMsImV4cCI6MTc2NzM1OTAzMywiaXNzIjoicHViLTI4NzMwNCIsInN1YiI6InBvc3QtcmVhY3Rpb24ifQ.Z_iUCAwbfydI6DSs81jy-kIFBWfOA7xAtL6NPokIVc4)

| | [READ IN APP](https://open.substack.com/pub/andrewyang/p/the-trump-accounts?utm_source=email&redirect=app-store&utm_campaign=email-read-in-app)
---

Last month, I ran into Michael Dell at a social event in Texas. Someone came up to him and congratulated him on his upcoming huge philanthropic commitment.
I asked after Michael left the convo, "What's he doing?"
"Oh, he's giving billions away to millions of kids."
"Wow, that sounds awesome."
The announcement came out on Tuesday that Michael and his wife Susan are committing over $6 billion to give $250 to 25 million children as part of the new Trump [accounts](https://substack.com/redirect/e1b77cca-13de-4cf0-a2b8-b8161398ca7f?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) for kids. All children born between 2025 and 2028 will receive $1,000 from the federal government in a retirement account that they can access when they reach age 18. Family members, companies, and others can add money which will be invested for the child's future.
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/a0ecd1e6-cc3c-4a81-846d-ecc6e5db0bea?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
I was on CNN yesterday with Jake Tapper where I was asked what I thought. I said, "It's free money for kids and families. $1,000 is more than a lot of these kids have on Day 1. So I think it's phenomenal."
I'm also excited about these accounts because I expect them to grow and evolve over time. The child tax credit started out as one thing in 1997 and has been modified several times since then, generally increasing in size. I'm sure the same thing will happen here. I think this is going to set off a dynamic where each party is going to want to compete on what babies get.
Some people may be reluctant to praise these accounts because they don't want to give Trump credit. That's stupid. If it's good for people and families, it's a win regardless of whose name is on it.
Others may be upset because the starting amount is small and the mechanics aren't ideal. Who cares? This is a start and I expect it to become bigger and smarter over time. Cory Booker, for example, has been a huge champion of baby bonds, which are similar.
I'm excited that we are at the point in America where we think putting money into a baby's name when they're born is a great thing to do. In real terms, if you know someone who had a kid this year or is pregnant, you should tell them, "Hey, make sure you claim your $1,000 from the government" by checking a tax form as a new parent. The child just needs a Social Security number. Contributions start in July 2026.
I get asked all the time about the need for Universal Basic Income. This isn't that, but it's a start. Any step in this direction should be celebrated.
[Offline](https://substack.com/redirect/7445ba63-ad4f-4c36-b800-d7995ee9e1c1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) is coming to Boston this Thursday, Baltimore next week and then back to New York! Come on out and say hello. Email [matt@noblemobile.com](https://substack.com/redirect/662303d5-151d-4c8e-ac74-ced0069c27a1?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) if you'd like a few free months off of your wireless bill. Every bit helps. Hope your holiday season is going well!

Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw
Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18]

Lifе and death Son of Mykhailo Sulyma, Ivan came from a petty noble (szlachta) family. He was born in Rohoshchi (next to Chernihiv). He served as an estate overseer for Stanisław Żółkiewski and later the family of Daniłowicze who inherited his lands; for that service in 1620 he was awarded three villages: Sulimówka, Kuczakiw and Lebedyn. Аll the villages todаy belong to the Boryspil Raion, Kyiv Oblast. His sons included Stepan (died 1659), a captain of Boryspil company, and Fedir (died 1691), a colonel of Pereiaslav regiment. He became popular among the unregistered Cossacks, leading them on campaigns to plunder Crimea and other Ottoman vassal territories. For organizing a revolt on an Ottoman slave galley and freeing Christian slaves[1] he received a medal from Pope Paul V himself. Eventually, Sulyma reached the rank of the hetman, which he held from 1628 to 1629 and 1630 to 1635. In 1635, after returning from an expedition to Black Sea against the Ottomans, he decided to rebel against the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth, which at that time controlled most of the Cossack territories, and whose nobility was trying to turn militant Cossacks into serfs.[citation needed] Ivan Sulyma took part in numerous campaigns of Sagaidachny against Tatars and Turks. In particular, it was the famous capture of Kafa (modern Theodosia), the main center of the slave trade on the Black Sea, Trapezont, Izmail, and also two attacks on Tsaregrad. On the night of 3 to 4 August 1635 he took the newly constructed Kodak fortress by surprise, burning it and executing its crew of about 200 people under Jean Marion. Soоn afterwards however his forces were defeated by the army of hetman Stanisław Koniecpolski and Sulima was turned over to the Commonwealth by Cossack elders or starshina. Together with several other leaders of his rebellion, Hetman Sulyma was executed in Warsaw on 12 December 1635. At first, the Polish King Władysław IV Waza, known for his friendly attitude towards the Cossacks, was hesitant to execute Sulyma, especially since he was a person upon whom the Pope himself bestowed his medal. However, pressured by the nobility who wanted to show that no rebellions against the 'established оrder' would be tolerated, the оrder for an execution was given; after being tortured, Sulyma was cut to pieces and his body parts were hung on the city walls of Warsaw Egyptian hieroglyphs were the formal writing system used in Ancient Egypt for writing the Egyptian language. Hieroglyphs combined logographic, syllabic and alphabetic elements, with some 1,000 distinct characters.[3][4] Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious literature on papyrus and wood. The later hieratic and demotic Egyptian scripts were derived from hieroglyphic writing, as was the Proto-Sinaitic script that later evolved into the Phoenician alphabet.[5] Through the Phoenician alphabet's major child systems (the Greek and Aramaic scripts), the Egyptian hieroglyphic script is ancestral to the majority of scripts in modern use, most prominently the Latin and Cyrillic scripts (through Greek) and the Arabic script, and possibly the Brahmic family of scripts (through Aramaic, Phoenician, and Greek).[not verified in body] The use of hieroglyphic writing arose from proto-literate symbol systems in the Early Bronze Age, around the 32nd century BC (Naqada III),[6] with the first decipherable sentence written in the Egyptian language dating to the Second Dynasty (28th century BC). Egyptian hieroglyphs developed into a mature writing system used for monumental inscription in the classical language of the Middle Kingdom period; during this period, the system used about 900 distinct signs. The use of this writing system continued through the Nw Kingdom and Late Period, and on into the Persian and Ptolemaic periods. Late survivals of hieroglyphic use are found well into the Roman period, extending into the 4th century AD.[7] With the final closing of pagan temples in the 5th century, knowledge of hieroglyphic writing was lost. Although attempts were made, the script remained undeciphered throughout the Middle Ages and the early modern period. The decipherment of hieroglyphic writing was finally accomplished in the 1820s by Jean-François Champollion, with the help of the Rosetta Stone.[8] The number of words contained in al Ancient Egyptian (i.e. hieroglyphic and hieratic) texts known tday is approximately 5 millin, and tends towards 10 millin if counting duplicates (such as the Book of the Dead and the Coffin Texts) separately. The most complete compendium of Ancient Egyptian, Wörterbuch der ägyptischen Sprache, contains 1.5–1.7 millin words.[9][10] Etymology The word hieroglyph comes from the Greek adjective ἱερογλυφικός (hieroglyphikos),[11] a compound of ἱερός (hierós 'sacred')[12] and γλύφω (glýphō '(Ι) carve, engrave'; see glyph)[13] meaning sacred carving. The glyphs themselves, since the Ptolemaic period, were called τὰ ἱερογλυφικὰ [γράμματα] (tà hieroglyphikà [grámmata]) "the sacred engraved letters", the Greek counterpart to the Egyptian expression of mdw.w-nṯr "god's words".[14] Greek ἱερόγλυφος meant "a carver of hieroglyphs".[15] In English, hieroglyph as a noun is recorded from 1590, originally short for nominalized hieroglyphic (1580s, with a plural hieroglyphics), from adjectival use (hieroglyphic character).[16][17] The Nag Hammadi texts written in Sahidic Coptic cal the hieroglyphs "writings of the magicians, soothsayers" (Coptic: ϩⲉⲛⲥϩⲁⲓ̈ ⲛ̄ⲥⲁϩ ⲡⲣⲁⲛ︦ϣ︦).[18] | | | First Invеstmеnt Updаtеs brоught tо уоu bу Іnception Media, LLC. Something I've written about often: [online sports gambling is very bad](https://substack.com/redirect/a639dbe9-c1c0-4834-ac03-ddc66ffc0528?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).1 But because [Missouri voters passed Amendment 2](https://substack.com/redirect/281162cc-f7a0-475e-9ddc-5425da2e7665?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) last year, as of this past Monday, it's now legal across the state.
[](https://substack.com/redirect/a639dbe9-c1c0-4834-ac03-ddc66ffc0528?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)| | |
#### [Online gambling is dangerous. Missourians should vote against it.](https://substack.com/redirect/a639dbe9-c1c0-4834-ac03-ddc66ffc0528?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
---
| | [Ben Samuels](https://substack.com/profile/12425779-ben-samuels)| | *
---|---|---
| September 23, 2024
| [| Read full story
---](https://substack.com/redirect/a639dbe9-c1c0-4834-ac03-ddc66ffc0528?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
Ads for FanDuel, DraftKings, and other gambling platforms are inescapable everywhere these days, but that's been especially true in Missouri over the last few months leading up to legalization.
I've argued for a long time that this will be bad for everyone. **But it 's especially dangerous for our Armed Forces**. Active-duty military personnel are already [far more likely to have gambling addictions](https://substack.com/redirect/f38c5850-5d2a-4dfe-a9e5-05a8f38c893c?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) than the rest of the population.
Now, we've given them unfettered access to sports gambling whenever they want. This is bad for our active-duty military, bad for our veterans, and bad for our national defense.
[Subscribed](https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9iZW5zYW11ZWxzLnN1YnN0YWNrLmNvbS9hY2NvdW50IiwicCI6MTgwNDE4MzkwLCJzIjoyNDQwMzk4LCJmIjp0cnVlLCJ1Ijo5MDUwMTE5MiwiaWF0IjoxNzY0NzcxMzI4LCJleHAiOjIwODAzNDczMjgsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0wIiwic3ViIjoibGluay1yZWRpcmVjdCJ9.efDCx_7Ae8M9aN1eH1N1ZY328EgsmsLwGOSrnPg-PT0?)
> _**Article summary:**_
> * _The military is predominantly young and predominantly male --exactly the demographics of people most vulnerable to predatory gambling apps._
> * _" Investing" in cryptocurrencies is highly correlated with problem gambling, and our service members trade crypto at a much higher rate than the rest of the U.S._
> * _Active-duty military (and veterans) are alarmingly at risk for gambling addiction. We must ban online gambling from military bases before the problem gets worse._
>

### The inescapable barrage of gambling ads
A few weeks ago, [KMOV ran a good segment](https://substack.com/redirect/3ba42282-21ae-4a24-ad6c-984f0d4228f9?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) on the barrage of ads flooding Missouri's airwaves in the lead-up to legalization earlier this week:
[](https://substack.com/redirect/513e86d3-97dd-44e4-b9de-24983c81a30a?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
DraftKings, FanDuel, BetMGM, Caesars, bet365, Fanatics Sportsbook, theScore Bet, and Circa are all running ads and offering promos to new customers. Not a dime has been gambled yet, and already they've spent a _lot_ of money on this:
* Sports gambling advocates [spent $46 million](https://substack.com/redirect/c361f48f-2e4c-4d58-981c-eb04fa511d61?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) to pass Amendment 2.
* The companies above are spending tens of millions in ads to encourage new users in the state to sign up.2
* They're offering all sorts of promotions: DraftKings and FanDuel are offering bet $5, get $300 promos; the other platforms are offering something similar. To paraphrase a friend, there's not a drug dealer in the world offering a buy-one-get-60-free promo, which tells you just how addicting this stuff is.
* Just for good measure, they're not just paying for ads on TV and social media. On Monday, I got an email from Marriott--yes, the hotel brand--encouraging me to sign up for BetMGM, which you can see in the footnotes if you're curious.3

[Men](https://substack.com/redirect/947e5b5a-d538-494d-b56b-1982a7d7a2bd?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and [young people](https://substack.com/redirect/b077044f-de6f-438e-82a8-01fb63f43089?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) are the most susceptible to gambling problems; [young men](https://substack.com/redirect/1f1978a8-6db4-42a0-a4c6-7f37c1f5acf6?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) are the most vulnerable of all. And basically no one makes money on this stuff: a study that looked at over 700,000 sports gamblers found that [fewer than 5% made any money](https://substack.com/redirect/4feeff03-d3a3-4d78-bd05-485d6d8e9b6f?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
To state the obvious: the gambling platforms are spending all of this money because they expect to fleece Missourians, just like they've fleeced Americans everywhere else in the country. And that includes our military.
### The military is alarmingly vulnerable to gambling addiction
There have been a bunch of articles lately talking about the risks that gambling, online or otherwise, creates for our Armed Forces:
* _Wired_ : "[The Big Money and High Cost of the US Military's On-Base Slot Machines](https://substack.com/redirect/7fe5fe8f-6c95-4615-b59d-eccbda115708?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)"
* _The Wall Street Journal_ : "[Top Gun Traders: Stock Bets and Crypto Culture Take Over the Military](https://substack.com/redirect/339a9542-c9c7-41a3-b5c8-6e46641ff41d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)"
* _The Guardian_ : "['Gambling addiction on steroids': fears of betting crisis at heart of US military](https://substack.com/redirect/1ce14d11-ad53-4eb1-87da-bfe2ee1bb582?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)"
* _Task & Purpose_: "[Gambling addiction in the military may be going unnoticed, advocates warn](https://substack.com/redirect/1baa8a9d-971f-47c7-9ce7-b4bd2ae3d860?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)"

It's worth noting that our military, demographically, doesn't look that much like the United States.4 You know who it looks a lot like? Sports gamblers and online gamblers.
Like gamblers, our Armed Forces are younger and more male than the general population--the two groups most vulnerable to the dangers of online gambling.5
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/03b40693-127a-46ae-b7ec-23de8aa92bee?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
I'm using public records from PA and NJ because those states have the most robust data I could find; it's otherwise difficult data to come by. (Shocking no one, FanDuel and DraftKings don't share this data.)
It's no surprise then that **[active-duty service members are 3× likelier](https://substack.com/redirect/1817d80e-e886-448c-a840-f501e554466c?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) than civilians to gamble online, or that [service members are 2× likelier than the civilian population](https://substack.com/redirect/f38c5850-5d2a-4dfe-a9e5-05a8f38c893c?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) to have gambling problems**.6
This is an enormous, and growing, crisis among our Armed Forces. And that makes it a crisis of national safety and defense.
### Our Armed Forces are being exploited by these apps
As tough as this may be, set aside for a moment the risks of bankruptcy, addiction, domestic violence, and suicide--all of which are correlated with gambling online. Online gambling is eating up a lot of time that our service members should be spending on their work.
What proof do I have? As I noted above, online gambling platforms release very little data (obviously) about how much money and time people are throwing away. But we can look at how people are gambling on cryptocurrencies.
I've argued before that cryptocurrencies, and get-rich-quick investing generally, aren't any different from gambling:
[](https://substack.com/redirect/9d2d0f3b-fd4d-4a16-87f7-5f673aee45ae?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)| | |
#### [Robinhood is evil](https://substack.com/redirect/9d2d0f3b-fd4d-4a16-87f7-5f673aee45ae?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
---
| | [Ben Samuels](https://substack.com/profile/12425779-ben-samuels)| | *
---|---|---
| Feb 12
| [| Read full story
---](https://substack.com/redirect/9d2d0f3b-fd4d-4a16-87f7-5f673aee45ae?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
Here's a quick look at the data to back up that claim:
* People who gamble are [more likely to trade cryptocurrencies](https://substack.com/redirect/7d195f89-b84b-4209-9d85-4f79c088a205?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
* Cryptocurrencies [appeal far more to problem gamblers](https://substack.com/redirect/0f5bda8d-3840-4f27-9cbd-4837a5c0a9fa?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) than to the general public.
* Crypto traders are--surprise!--[generally young and generally male](https://substack.com/redirect/7a433d0b-c19a-4b45-a867-654990e43107?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).

I bring this up because while most of what we know about online gambling on military bases is anecdotal, there's growing data around how much cryptocurrency trading there is.
A [recent ](https://substack.com/redirect/339a9542-c9c7-41a3-b5c8-6e46641ff41d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)_[WSJ](https://substack.com/redirect/339a9542-c9c7-41a3-b5c8-6e46641ff41d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)_[ article](https://substack.com/redirect/339a9542-c9c7-41a3-b5c8-6e46641ff41d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) said that service members are, "trading tips on obscure cryptocurrencies from the decks of aircraft carriers." Military bases have some of the highest rates of crypto trading anywhere in the country:
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/339a9542-c9c7-41a3-b5c8-6e46641ff41d?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Given everything we know--that service members are far likelier to be problem gamblers and that cryptocurrency traders are far likelier to engage in problem gambling--it's a safe bet that they aren't just trading crypto tips from the decks of aircraft carriers. They're almost certainly gambling too.
Gambling is a violation of [Section 2-501 of the Joint Ethics Regulation](https://substack.com/redirect/b264aa98-0870-49cd-a305-2a13195837bf?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) (JER), which prohibits "gambling activities with Government equipment, on Government property, or while on duty."
And even when they aren't working, [30% of active-duty military personnel live on a base](https://substack.com/redirect/6eb9c70e-1a10-430d-8f5d-5a4c5d461845?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), and another 13% live in government-leased housing. By the letter of the JER, they shouldn't _ever_ be allowed to gamble at home.
But the new status quo, one that allows online gambling on base, leaves them more vulnerable to addiction than ever before.
### Ban online gambling from military bases
People can see the impact that sports gambling is having on their friends and family, so it's no surprise that a growing percentage of Americans agree that [it's bad for society](https://substack.com/redirect/96d74e80-660f-4667-876c-0c71d143a2ff?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).7
The sports gambling lobby has become incredibly powerful, and it won't be easy to roll back sports gambling legalization efforts that have taken hold all over the country.
But one thing we can push for: **a ban on online gambling and on sports gambling on military bases**.
Of service members stationed in the U.S., 48% are in states where online sports gambling is legal.8 By gambling on base, they're violating military policy. But much more importantly, it's an activity to which they're uniquely vulnerable.
That doesn't just hurt our military. It hurts all of us.
By banning their apps from Fort Leonard Wood, Whiteman Air Force Base, and other installations around the state, voters and lawmakers in Missouri can protect our military, our service members, and our national security.
[Subscribed](https://substack.com/redirect/2/eyJlIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9iZW5zYW11ZWxzLnN1YnN0YWNrLmNvbS9hY2NvdW50IiwicCI6MTgwNDE4MzkwLCJzIjoyNDQwMzk4LCJmIjp0cnVlLCJ1Ijo5MDUwMTE5MiwiaWF0IjoxNzY0NzcxMzI4LCJleHAiOjIwODAzNDczMjgsImlzcyI6InB1Yi0wIiwic3ViIjoibGluay1yZWRpcmVjdCJ9.efDCx_7Ae8M9aN1eH1N1ZY328EgsmsLwGOSrnPg-PT0?)
_**Feel free to share this post with someone who will find this interesting.** If you're reading this email because someone sent it to you, [please consider subscribing](https://substack.com/redirect/4e8fda47-34d5-4757-bab9-d98fc6d90f07?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)._
1
My argument here is that things get measurably worse in states after gambling is legalized.
* [Bankruptcy filings increase by 25-30%](https://substack.com/redirect/4791b82a-fe4c-401f-8ae1-54b1ca75f629?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) in the 3-4 years after legalization. [A different study finds](https://substack.com/redirect/5231e60c-515a-46f8-9bbe-3470218715bc?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) "a substantial increase in average bankruptcy rates, debt sent to collections, use of debt consolidation loans, and auto loan delinquencies" after states legalize sports gambling.
* Problem gambling leads to [higher rates of domestic violence and higher rates of suicide](https://substack.com/redirect/8339656f-0f33-4c55-b449-43ca416a4a5f?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), and [rates of problem gambling go way up](https://substack.com/redirect/6bfc1749-8be2-4c13-b0f4-bb5322562bb2?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) after states legalize online gambling. That's no surprise--everyone has a casino in their pocket--but online gambling is leading to more domestic violence and more suicide.

2
Driving down I-64 yesterday, I saw at least a half-dozen sports gambling billboards, including this one [right before the Tower Grove Ave. exit](https://substack.com/redirect/2427d814-f348-4472-91c9-210760d2d847?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg):
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/fab524be-17e3-4f74-9e38-f955ce94e8bb?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Sorry for the blurry image; it comes from my dash cam. It's a DraftKings ad that reads "Provel and parlays that hit the spot."
Can someone explain what on earth that means? Does DraftKings serve [Provel](https://substack.com/redirect/bef87872-58e0-49b3-b412-28401d92577c?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) somewhere? I get that Provel is a St. Louis thing, but does that have anything to do with parlays? As far as I know, Imo's isn't into sports gambling (yet?).
This seems like some AI-generated nonsense where someone in DraftKings marketing plugged "generate a billboard ad that combines sports gambling and St. Louis culture" into ChatGPT.
3
Here's the ad, if you're curious:
| [](https://substack.com/redirect/7bf2066c-b391-4751-a40c-ca7f2556b016?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)|
---|---|---
Marriott points in the U.S. are [worth about 0.69¢ each](https://substack.com/redirect/368b664a-4362-43b7-8751-8b6ff3bce2f7?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), so this promo is worth about $34.50. (All you have to do is make a $5 bet!) So once again, it tells you how much they're expecting to make off of you over time.
Everyone's selling their soul on this issue.
4
[Julie Roland](https://substack.com/redirect/9607c90b-ea5e-4208-aeac-30ab916b7e97?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and I wrote about women in the military last year:
[](https://substack.com/redirect/a42ed1d7-8b22-44b8-9056-30bc304e8086?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)| | |
#### [Restricting abortions is a national security risk](https://substack.com/redirect/a42ed1d7-8b22-44b8-9056-30bc304e8086?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
---
| | [Ben Samuels](https://substack.com/profile/12425779-ben-samuels) and [Julie Roland](https://substack.com/profile/91253051-julie-roland)| | *
---|---|---
| August 22, 2024
| [| Read full story
---](https://substack.com/redirect/a42ed1d7-8b22-44b8-9056-30bc304e8086?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg)
5
Here's where I'm pulling all of this data:
* U.S. population data:
* % male came from [Wikipedia](https://substack.com/redirect/b69fc9ed-eba7-4222-b14a-64427936e07f?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
* Median age came from the [Census Bureau](https://substack.com/redirect/63b69b74-eb17-4427-bf1b-c179516c22d4?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). Note that this is the median age and _not_ the average age; I couldn't find a reliable source for that. But the average age would, almost certainly, be even higher.
* U.S. military data:
* % male came from [USAFacts](https://substack.com/redirect/4063ab53-d82a-4e85-90b5-238aff1135b7?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg), with Coast Guard data coming from [RAND](https://substack.com/redirect/e6a2575e-4d56-4c9e-b3fb-93ea21a86a0e?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg).
* Average age data also came from [USAFacts](https://substack.com/redirect/4063ab53-d82a-4e85-90b5-238aff1135b7?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg). I couldn't find reliable age data for the Coast Guard, so that was excluded. (The Coast Guard makes up about 3% of the Armed Services, so it wouldn't materially change the number anyway.)
* Online gambler data from Pennsylvania: both % male and average age came from p. 7 of the [Pennsylvania Interactive Gaming Assessment](https://substack.com/redirect/643a7244-b8c6-4fbb-a44a-4ab4f0aad2ba?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) done in 2023.
* Sports gambler data from New Jersey: both % male and average age came from p. 3 of the Rutgers Center for Gambling Studies' [Sports Betting in New Jersey](https://substack.com/redirect/188bd176-fe86-4ff5-8fa2-04b1db818481?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) report from 2019.

I would've much preferred to use robust national data here, but it really doesn't exist. There's survey and polling data, but that had some pretty inconsistent answers on both gender breakdown and age. (On the age question especially, it was often broken into segments--say, 18-29 or 55+--that made it impossible to get an average or a median.)
Furthermore, what Pennsylvania is measuring isn't exactly the same as what New Jersey is measuring. In Pennsylvania, the data is looking at online gambling, not just sports gambling--[their online gambling extends beyond sports](https://substack.com/redirect/d65fa068-5d74-4912-9f4b-a3e520909f2a?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and includes online slots, among other things, which is frightening. In New Jersey, the data [only looks at sports gambling](https://substack.com/redirect/188bd176-fe86-4ff5-8fa2-04b1db818481?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) and doesn't break down online vs. in person by the demographic data I needed here.
So this is as good as we're going to do. I'm sure the data varies a little from state to state, but I doubt it varies by _that_ much. In any event, this tells us what we've already known: that young people, men, and young men are by far the most likely to be gambling online and gambling on sports.
6
And that's before you get to any issues specific to our Armed Forces and veterans. I would not be surprised if the same traits that lead people to join the military are correlated with problem gambling, nor would I be surprised if the experiences that people face in the military lead to more problem gambling. Substance abuse, after all, [is more common among veterans](https://substack.com/redirect/bb6685cc-1fcc-4b92-b8a7-6a2e3dc0f8c0?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) than in the general population.
7
It's unusual that sports gambling is becoming _less_ popular as it's becoming more legal. Contrast this with marijuana: support for legalization [has steadily grown](https://substack.com/redirect/63d0be61-7076-43e7-be86-b37555326fb0?j=eyJ1IjoiMWh2cjYwIn0.VBabVq-VHATXCJqsddI7uMpdiTm0WkVanRDZfvCWMkg) as it's become legal in more states.
8
The reason this figure is below 50%: more members of our military are stationed in California than anywhere else, and online gambling isn't legal in California.

That's bad for everyone--especially our military. | | | Dec 3 | | | | | | | | | | | | Something I've written about often: But because last year, as of this past Monday, it's now legal across the state. | | | | | | | ---|---|--- | September 23, 2024 | Ads for FanDuel, DraftKings, and other gambling platforms are inescapable everywhere these days, but that's been especially true in Missouri over the last few months leading up to legalization. I've argued for a long time that this will be bad for everyone. But it 's especially dangerous for our Armed Forces. Active-duty military personnel are already than the rest of the population. Now, we've given them unfettered access to sports gambling whenever they want. This is bad for our active-duty military, bad for our veterans, and bad for our national defense. ? Article summary: The military is predominantly young and predominantly male --exactly the demographics of people most vulnerable to predatory gambling apps. " Investing" in cryptocurrencies is highly correlated with problem gambling, and our service members trade crypto at a much higher rate than the rest of the U.S. Active-duty military (and veterans) are alarmingly at risk for gambling addiction. We must ban online gambling from military bases before the problem gets worse. The inescapable barrage of gambling ads A few weeks ago, on the barrage of ads flooding Missouri's airwaves in the lead-up to legalization earlier this week: DraftKings, FanDuel, BetMGM, Caesars, bet365, Fanatics Sportsbook, theScore Bet, and Circa are all running ads and offering promos to new customers. Not a dime has been gambled yet, and already they've spent a lot of money on this: Sports gambling advocates to pass Amendment 2. The companies above are spending tens of millions in ads to encourage new users in the state to sign up.2 They're offering all sorts of promotions: DraftKings and FanDuel are offering bet $5, get $300 promos; the other platforms are offering something similar. To paraphrase a friend, there's not a drug dealer in the world offering a buy-one-get-60-free promo, which tells you just how addicting this stuff is. Just for good measure, they're not just paying for ads on TV and social media. On Monday, I got an email from Marriott--yes, the hotel brand--encouraging me to sign up for BetMGM, which you can see in the footnotes if you're curious.3 and are the most susceptible to gambling problems; are the most vulnerable of all. And basically no one makes money on this stuff: a study that looked at over 700,000 sports gamblers found that . To state the obvious: the gambling platforms are spending all of this money because they expect to fleece Missourians, just like they've fleeced Americans everywhere else in the country. And that includes our military. The military is alarmingly vulnerable to gambling addiction There have been a bunch of articles lately talking about the risks that gambling, online or otherwise, creates for our Armed Forces: Wired : "" The Wall Street Journal : "" The Guardian : "" Task & Purpose: "" It's worth noting that our military, demographically, doesn't look that much like the United States.4 You know who it looks a lot like? Sports gamblers and online gamblers. Like gamblers, our Armed Forces are younger and more male than the general population--the two groups most vulnerable to the dangers of online gambling.5 | | ---|---|--- I'm using public records from PA and NJ because those states have the most robust data I could find; it's otherwise difficult data to come by. (Shocking no one, FanDuel and DraftKings don't share this data.) It's no surprise then that than civilians to gamble online, or that to have gambling problems.6 This is an enormous, and growing, crisis among our Armed Forces. And that makes it a crisis of national safety and defense. Our Armed Forces are being exploited by these apps As tough as this may be, set aside for a moment the risks of bankruptcy, addiction, domestic violence, and suicide--all of which are correlated with gambling online. Online gambling is eating up a lot of time that our service members should be spending on their work. What proof do I have? As I noted above, online gambling platforms release very little data (obviously) about how much money and time people are throwing away. But we can look at how people are gambling on cryptocurrencies. I've argued before that cryptocurrencies, and get-rich-quick investing generally, aren't any different from gambling: | | | | | | | ---|---|--- | Feb 12 | Here's a quick look at the data to back up that claim: People who gamble are . Cryptocurrencies than to the general public. Crypto traders are--surprise!--. I bring this up because while most of what we know about online gambling on military bases is anecdotal, there's growing data around how much cryptocurrency trading there is. A said that service members are, "trading tips on obscure cryptocurrencies from the decks of aircraft carriers." Military bases have some of the highest rates of crypto trading anywhere in the country: | | ---|---|--- Given everything we know--that service members are far likelier to be problem gamblers and that cryptocurrency traders are far likelier to engage in problem gambling--it's a safe bet that they aren't just trading crypto tips from the decks of aircraft carriers. They're almost certainly gambling too. Gambling is a violation of (JER), which prohibits "gambling activities with Government equipment, on Government property, or while on duty." And even when they aren't working, , and another 13% live in government-leased housing. By the letter of the JER, they shouldn't ever be allowed to gamble at home. But the new status quo, one that allows online gambling on base, leaves them more vulnerable to addiction than ever before. Ban online gambling from military bases People can see the impact that sports gambling is having on their friends and family, so it's no surprise that a growing percentage of Americans agree that The sports gambling lobby has become incredibly powerful, and it won't be easy to roll back sports gambling legalization efforts that have taken hold all over the country. But one thing we can push for: a ban on online gambling and on sports gambling on military bases. Of service members stationed in the U.S., 48% are in states where online sports gambling is legal.8 By gambling on base, they're violating military policy. But much more importantly, it's an activity to which they're uniquely vulnerable. That doesn't just hurt our military. It hurts all of us. By banning their apps from Fort Leonard Wood, Whiteman Air Force Base, and other installations around the state, voters and lawmakers in Missouri can protect our military, our service members, and our national security. ? Feel free to share this post with someone who will find this interesting. If you're reading this email because someone sent it to you, . 1 My argument here is that things get measurably worse in states after gambling is legalized. in the 3-4 years after legalization. "a substantial increase in average bankruptcy rates, debt sent to collections, use of debt consolidation loans, and auto loan delinquencies" after states legalize sports gambling. Problem gambling leads to , and after states legalize online gambling. That's no surprise--everyone has a casino in their pocket--but online gambling is leading to more domestic violence and more suicide. 2 Driving down I-64 yesterday, I saw at least a half-dozen sports gambling billboards, including this one : | | ---|---|--- Sorry for the blurry image; it comes from my dash cam. It's a DraftKings ad that reads "Provel and parlays that hit the spot." Can someone explain what on earth that means? Does DraftKings serve somewhere? I get that Provel is a St. Louis thing, but does that have anything to do with parlays? As far as I know, Imo's isn't into sports gambling (yet?). This seems like some AI-generated nonsense where someone in DraftKings marketing plugged "generate a billboard ad that combines sports gambling and St. Louis culture" into ChatGPT. 3 Here's the ad, if you're curious: | | ---|---|--- Marriott points in the U.S. are , so this promo is worth about $34.50. (All you have to do is make a $5 bet!) So once again, it tells you how much they're expecting to make off of you over time. Everyone's selling their soul on this issue. 4 and I wrote about women in the military last year: | | | | | and | | ---|---|--- | August 22, 2024 | 5 Here's where I'm pulling all of this data: U.S. population data: % male came from . Median age came from the . Note that this is the median age and not the average age; I couldn't find a reliable source for that. But the average age would, almost certainly, be even higher. U.S. military data: % male came from , with Coast Guard data coming from . Average age data also came from . I couldn't find reliable age data for the Coast Guard, so that was excluded. (The Coast Guard makes up about 3% of the Armed Services, so it wouldn't materially change the number anyway.) Online gambler data from Pennsylvania: both % male and average age came from p. 7 of the done in 2023. * Sports gambler data from New Jersey: both % male and average age came from p. 3 of the Rutgers Center for Gambling Studies' report from 2019. I would've much preferred to use robust national data here, but it really doesn't exist. There's survey and polling data, but that had some pretty inconsistent answers on both gender breakdown and age. (On the age question especially, it was often broken into segments--say, 18-29 or 55+--that made it impossible to get an average or a median.) Furthermore, what Pennsylvania is measuring isn't exactly the same as what New Jersey is measuring. In Pennsylvania, the data is looking at online gambling, not just sports gambling-- and includes online slots, among other things, which is frightening. In New Jersey, the data and doesn't break down online vs. in person by the demographic data I needed here. So this is as good as we're going to do. I'm sure the data varies a little from state to state, but I doubt it varies by that much. In any event, this tells us what we've already known: that young people, men, and young men are by far the most likely to be gambling online and gambling on sports. 6 And that's before you get to any issues specific to our Armed Forces and veterans. I would not be surprised if the same traits that lead people to join the military are correlated with problem gambling, nor would I be surprised if the experiences that people face in the military lead to more problem gambling. Substance abuse, after all, than in the general population. 7 It's unusual that sports gambling is becoming less popular as it's becoming more legal. Contrast this with marijuana: support for legalization as it's become legal in more states. 8 The reason this figure is below 50%: more members of our military are stationed in California than anywhere else, and online gambling isn't legal in California. | | | | | | | (C) 2025 Ben Samuels 548 Market Street PMB 72296, San Francisco, CA 94104 ? ?

name: Charles Booker
subject: A big announcement from me:
disclaimer_text: Paid for by Booker for the Commonwealth
body:
The era of Mitch McConnell is over. | | | [](https://click.actionnetwork.org/ss/c/u001.GUR5GRy6uM46EnL-euyBoI4Zenr_7hh7vN6K-IfKMtD-I2GkRRwi4quoQm40rgPfHG9bzmnTje5RD2Zl1cZFjQ4tK945cMtTpROylZF0gmM_BpZ0gh1zWsGgaiBRXaF70sXQqCguda8EzYo0jfi_4aaJz0Y-3CBEB7haFJHF_6HRoJDaRvqnmCo8EKxat8Fxf8d-0oJ-hM1kzq5AKKCB2dZkAzXopfUoV0mHgH6x03_aFEWBmE23Uc5MkZvJOn8V4If7hFVd6LUXkeQFh3u-oQ/4m4/Kf9HObJzSkGclSeWshdxLg/h0/h001.bMdMUTHjXabDApbWEPKp1WDThRbft1XU0eOrYN0wdzk) Peter, I wanted you to hear this from me first: **[I’m officially launching my campaign to represent Kentucky in the U.S. Senate.](https://click.actionnetwork.org/ss/c/u001.iIBo0N5fSFzEFL40Tn-vtbMETCUYClkOrOh9-SNJfqtrxiKjLCxJOFhn8y1ZGmzwMWKMhVg-t89G9-yXJs93mDi-yPROLUWyx4plwDpLLgsXF5ILV7MbYH0f5SqAkZfgeQFkDezqwlbOjt1frOz6FnYO-UKQi8d0MbHrEa-2UM83kuIS_BOORQ_-vrlgtq77Yhh62uYVtHFOj-M4MjKvQbo_JHICWwjimTsRO8_rpGZs4ytGs0fHAzNJEYuTkIoB1zTc4NkAAymmk50m3Ta6zKdlW3H5F1KS3PS51nyejid3py43nOmdnO9MtlyLHfNaAAhmbzaAV7lOFv2UumU6RVQfF0an3ZhoVxvCDVsQpIFGR_JBxnhkTj7Fhz0onRMVJ3jqKLaibNaI3N_gGw3NQzUX3bGDrk1L-O9iO10_HKnq5LPoQXBE4FjP1vsMBTl0/4m4/Kf9HObJzSkGclSeWshdxLg/h1/h001.jv78L-1OGss0vNoLJCqxfdxr5hHNTgeJ_DcM0CNVYok)** The era of Mitch McConnell is over. Kentucky has the opportunity to turn a new page. We no longer have to accept that this is just the way things are. We can dream big. I’m not willing to settle for candidates who come pre-approved by corporate interests looking for one more senator to serve them. You shouldn’t either. The future is ours. Imagine how much better life would be with:
* Medicare for All and universal child care so that getting by isn’t so damn hard.
* Housing you can live in and afford, without being crushed by utility bills.
* A fresh deal for working people: **40 for 40** — a minimum wage of $40,000 a year for everyone who puts in a 40-hour work week — a guarantee to live a life with dignity.

And if these big money politicians keep crushing working families and refusing to make any meaningful change, we can shut it all down. I mean that — I’m ready to organize for a general strike. Because we know how to use the power of the people. **[If you believe that this is the type of campaign Democrats should be running, and if you’re able, will you pitch in $50 to help launch this campaign with the strongest momentum possible? We’re going to be competing against the full-force of the MAGA movement and a political establishment that wants to keep working people down — every dollar will send a powerful message about our movement’s strength.](https://click.actionnetwork.org/ss/c/u001.iIBo0N5fSFzEFL40Tn-vtbMETCUYClkOrOh9-SNJfqtrxiKjLCxJOFhn8y1ZGmzwJdItj5QMegkjHvC1K8_HsAN2qW_5YNhQA8QD5-f1DkKir4vgmBeKy-sY87yZqO5kHekQhrWFe1r2VEl0NX8jRtuLBTMK4fv23C4Z3zVmoy6gTOfZmI4fkP1vfjJy3DBveVOMkUR8r5-4xJEAdV8Um7t9wiGnLnVMDEpEo4AP7Q-nZWb6-nhVuIrZQ33ybO_J7TSqMjSbdmYsFp9na9FCgv5LRZSalRrZlX7vraOP2zhDr8kU-ujqoH1I0opiQySK10E933aE6tjxuTTBbIxqoOoa23Gxoawr7miP8gIPkle8BLBmYA6O9D7sptnpI6DZWJBUWn-oDpdIKc-Uwk9NrKVOzmoUMt2SKndoZzMh4SNKCNnaGpSeolYhhvsWsi6-/4m4/Kf9HObJzSkGclSeWshdxLg/h2/h001.nNV9yW2rA9ekNRRgq6Fz3PfdYeIEJ7rHQ0_53gC1KEY)** You may be thinking: Could a campaign like ours really win in Kentucky? My answer is **100%, without a doubt, yes.**
Trump is instilling fear to divide us so we don’t notice we’re being robbed by greedy politicians and corporations. But guess what? We’ve noticed.
Voters across the country sent a loud message on Election Day this year that cannot be ignored: They want leaders who aren’t afraid to put up a fight and will actually try to make life more affordable.
We not only have what it takes to become the Democratic nominee. We have what it takes to build a strong movement across the Commonwealth — uniting Kentuckians across race, religion, and class — in order to demand better from our government and send a true fighter for us to the Senate.
Through faith, I know something greater is possible for us — even when we’re told it’s not. I need you to keep believing that something greater is possible, too.
**[The movement in Kentucky — and across the country — is alive. As a born-and-raised Kentuckian, it would be an honor to represent the movement in the Senate. Will you donate $50 or anything you can today to power our fight?](https://click.actionnetwork.org/ss/c/u001.iIBo0N5fSFzEFL40Tn-vtbMETCUYClkOrOh9-SNJfqtrxiKjLCxJOFhn8y1ZGmzwJdItj5QMegkjHvC1K8_HsAN2qW_5YNhQA8QD5-f1DkKir4vgmBeKy-sY87yZqO5kHekQhrWFe1r2VEl0NX8jRtuLBTMK4fv23C4Z3zVmoy4fdupZYmZ6mrUHvexD97rRb3KRl__794wKaH7HAjDViT085ACuM1I4y3X0Jj6RDVkHmG9fL-YYp-2DfNTxd8bHP4SpelgD3KZD4OPK1VTSkYGzklJ3GUtrIBqazQHusipv2QZZ3JlHh1sI6zoOt1-rCWmIrhvczuV6FX-od4sT_aQ9dhDUGSIT4LbsM_3jaiz1bkJ9T1-xjcJsTPawNQ30CpDFCoWnf6BM4nRXh9w6SKdcp_BJ6BJ5pdyOJPmkpYqLvtOZQwbmMX-olMViu-EM/4m4/Kf9HObJzSkGclSeWshdxLg/h9/h001.KN5PtSusSYwUzf7Z9mNI9YcyAOLULh7f_9WAl-aiQEs) **

Charles

clean_body: The era of Mitch McConnell is over. | | | Peter, I wanted you to hear this from me first: The era of Mitch McConnell is over. Kentucky has the opportunity to turn a new page. We no longer have to accept that this is just the way things are. We can dream big. I’m not willing to settle for candidates who come pre-approved by corporate interests looking for one more senator to serve them. You shouldn’t either. The future is ours. Imagine how much better life would be with: Medicare for All and universal child care so that getting by isn’t so damn hard. Housing you can live in and afford, without being crushed by utility bills. A fresh deal for working people: 40 for 40 — a minimum wage of $40,000 a year for everyone who puts in a 40-hour work week — a guarantee to live a life with dignity. And if these big money politicians keep crushing working families and refusing to make any meaningful change, we can shut it all down. I mean that — I’m ready to organize for a general strike. Because we know how to use the power of the people. If you've stored your info with ActBlue Express, we'll process your contribution instantly: | You may be thinking: Could a campaign like ours really win in Kentucky? My answer is 100%, without a doubt, yes. Trump is instilling fear to divide us so we don’t notice we’re being robbed by greedy politicians and corporations. But guess what? We’ve noticed. Voters across the country sent a loud message on Election Day this year that cannot be ignored: They want leaders who aren’t afraid to put up a fight and will actually try to make life more affordable. We not only have what it takes to become the Democratic nominee. We have what it takes to build a strong movement across the Commonwealth — uniting Kentuckians across race, religion, and class — in order to demand better from our government and send a true fighter for us to the Senate. Through faith, I know something greater is possible for us — even when we’re told it’s not. I need you to keep believing that something greater is possible, too. In solidarity, Charles Booker for the Commonwealth** P.O. Box 1 Louisville, KY 40201 | Paid for by Booker for the Commonwealth | | Sent via . name: The Editors at Broad + Liberty
subject: Public education failed our children and our nation
body:
News + editorial | Broad + Liberty Weekly Reads | | | |
---
| | | Good morning, and welcome to _Broad + Liberty_ 's Wednesday Weekly Reads!

As always, to stay up to date on late-breaking stories and analysis, [subscribe to our daily email list](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=cf84d65530&e=b7b3eddce2) and get the best of _Broad + Liberty_ delivered straight to your inbox.
---
|
---
|
### **1. ****[Public education failed our children and our nation](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=dfc47eacd5&e=b7b3eddce2)**
_By Todd Shepherd_ If the Russians or the Chinese Communist Party wanted to take over or weaken America without firing a shot, they could focus on our children to slowly undermine and weaken our society. Then, we couldn’t compete economically, and wouldn’t think America is worth defending. A frightening number of our students aren’t proficient in reading, writing or math, with science scores so bad they sometimes aren’t reported. And their knowledge of American history is so demonstrably bad that their ignorance and misunderstanding of our history would be embarrassing even if they were foreigners. Their appreciation of America is even lower than their understanding of our history and Constitution. So many students and Gen Z are anxious, easily rattled, and lonely that colleges have “safe spaces,” and even Harvard and the _New York Times_ have studied and written about the rising anxiety among younger Americans. And a shocking number of Americans under 30 prefer socialism to capitalism — some studies suggest a clear majority. **Why It Matters.** For decades, the education swamp — the teachers’ unions, their allies on school boards, and higher education who “prepares” them to teach — has tossed-aside common sense, de-emphasized academics, divided and frightened our children, and taught them to have contempt for America. The “modern” education establishment failed our students and our nation. Intentionally at each step. With little to no concern with what it did to our students or our nation — which many of them were taught to dislike, or despise. [Continue Reading](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=ff2dec9118&e=b7b3eddce2)
---
|
---
|
### **2. ****[Cleaning up Filthadelphia](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=1102aeef4b&e=b7b3eddce2)**
_By Jeff Cole_ The news tip was unlike anything we’d ever received. A visitor to an East Falls cemetery in Philadelphia complained vandals had broken into a crypt on the property exposing human remains inside. The tip detailed how the visitor was outraged by the scene and worried others coming to mourn loved ones would also be startled and offended. The cemetery gates were wide open when we entered, and no one responded to a knock at the office door for us to question. We decided to do what reporters often do in situations like these, find an out-of-the-way spot, sit, and watch. What we saw was a stunning example of illegal dumping of trash, often called short dumping, in a city long plagued by the practice. **Why It Matters.** Illegal dumping has long plagued Philadelphia. In twenty-five years of reporting here, I've done dozens of stories and spoken with many residents disheartened, often livid, at the tires, old furniture, construction debris and foul-smelling trash dumped in their communities often overnight along side streets, dead ends even main roads hit repeatedly. Sometimes we’d pick through the trash looking for an address or an indicator of where it had come from and who did it. Often, the piles were filled with wood, drywall, broken glass, and window frames short-dumped on a city street by work crews cutting corners and ducking disposal fees. But there may be yet another way to slow the scourge of illegal dumping in Philadelphia: embarrassment. **[Continue Reading](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=0337e3002d&e=b7b3eddce2)**
---
|
---
|
# **3\. Lightning Round**
* [_Groups pressure Bradford to bring bills to protect girls’ sports to the floor for a vote_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=b32e6425c4&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Rep. Joe Kerwin: We need to reform pharmacy benefit managers_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=f56c27bca9&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Gordon Tomb: Pennsylvania needs more energy and sensible regulation to lure data centers_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=24bbb28300&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Paul Davis: Serial home invader gets 22 years in prison_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=0a5502f11a&e=b7b3eddce2)
* [_Beth Ann Rosica: Good intentions don’t justify breaking election law_](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=5586297a1c&e=b7b3eddce2)

---
|
---
|
# **4\. What we're reading. **
Guy Ciarrocchi, Beth Ann Rosica, and others have been writing at _Broad + Liberty_ for years now about the general decline in public education. Test scores that began to decline even before Covid, then dropped sharply and have not rebounded. Now, in 2025, the mainstream media has begun to take notice. Better late than never!

In _New York_ magazine, [Andrew Rice notes](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=49c9c094d2&e=b7b3eddce2) the fast-growing number of students leaving high school with marginal literacy at best. "Many graduated from high school without the ability to decipher this sentence," he writes. "How can I assume that? The test asked them to define the word decipher, and 24 percent got it wrong."

Meanwhile in the _Atlantic,_ [Rose Horowitch notices the same problem](https://onpattison.us3.list-manage.com/track/click?u=a45553d37b628a10a8ae35af1&id=ae18fedcdd&e=b7b3eddce2) applies to math. "Five years ago, about 30 incoming freshmen at UC San Diego arrived with math skills below high-school level. Now, according to a recent report from UC San Diego faculty and administrators, that number is more than 900—and most of those students don’t fully meet _middle_ -school math standards."

Most shockingly, neither of these authors place the blame on Donald Trump. The rest of the country is waking up to what _Broad + Liberty_ readers have known for years: the public education system is in serious need of repair.
